data.title: Broadcom's Expertise In SerDes And DSP Drives Success In An AI World
data.description:
Introduction

Broadcom (AVGO) builds Serializer/Deserializer (“SerDes”) into the chips they co-design with customers, enabling high-speed connections between them within AI clusters. The analog circuits in the SerDes receiver do the preliminary work of amplifying and shaping the degraded signal before handing it off to digital signal processing (“DSP”). In order to more fully reconstruct the received signal, DSP runs sophisticated algorithms.

Nvidia (NVDA) designs AI factories built around GPUs, networking, and software. Broadcom co-designs XPUs, which are a particular type of application-specific integrated circuit (“ASICs”). Ostensibly the TPUs Broadcom co-designs with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) are ASICs and XPUs, but Google is Broadcom’s largest customer, and their TPUs are often treated as a separate category. XPUs generally refer to the broad category of custom accelerators co-designed with Meta Platforms (META) and others.

My thesis is that Broadcom’s expertise in SerDes and DSP drives success in an AI world.

Broadcom’s fiscal 2025 went through November 2, 2025. As such, their fiscal quarters are almost two months ahead of calendar quarters. For example, fiscal Q1 2026 goes through February 1, 2026, instead of March 31, 2026.

SerDes And DSP

Broadcom is a leader in terms of serializing data from a chip, which is basically merging multiple wires/lanes. The data is then deserialized or unpacked as it arrives at the other end on another chip. Speed is critical, and power is a constraint. Part of the end of the SerDes process is DSP - the stage when the received signal is more fully cleaned and processed.

Physical Layer Products General Manager Vijay Janapaty explained why Broadcom is the world leader with SerDes at the Enabling AI Infrastructure event in March 2024. He explains how Broadcom had a history of driving the best SerDes in the industry for at least four generations leading up to the event. There are four key elements that explain this:

1. They have built up capability with high-speed analog.

2. Their DSP depth is custom-built for high-speed communication. Their algorithms for signal recovery are world-class.

3. Broadcom takes this know-how from #1 and #2 into leading-edge technology nodes such as 3-nm chips, and they work concurrently with TSMC (TSM). Being at the forefront with the foundry means they are first to market when new nodes launch.

4. Broadcom’s scale of operation is broad. They have deployed countless SerDes cores across hundreds of systems, continually learning from each generation.

General Manager Janapaty sums up these four elements in a slide:

More recently, on the Q1 2026 call, CEO Hock Tan said Broadcom’s SerDes moat is one of the reasons customers are drawn to them. Investors worry about the possibility of companies like Google eventually cutting Broadcom out of the loop and designing chips and clusters by themselves or with partners who offer better terms. JPMorgan Chase (JPM) Analyst Harlan Sur asked about the customer-owned tooling (“COT”) threat in the Q&A portion of the Q1 2026 call:

Now on AI, some of these COT initiatives are coming to the market now, but it looks like they're at least 2x less performant than your current generation solutions, 2x less complex in terms of chip design complexity, packaging complexity, IP. So maybe just a quick 2-part question. Hock, one for you is, given your visibility into next year, do you see these COT science projects taking any meaningful TPU, XPU share from Broadcom? And then maybe the second quick question for either you or Charlie is, given that Broadcom's TPU, XPU programs from a performance complexity IP perspective are 12 to 18 months ahead of any of these COT programs, how does the Broadcom team widen this gap further?

CEO Tan cites Broadcom’s cutting-edge SerDes capability in his answer about the challenges with respect to bringing initiatives to market:

You need cutting edge, really cutting-edge SerDes, very advanced packaging and most - and just as much, you need to understand how to network clusters of them together. We've been doing this for 20 years, more than 20 years in silicon. And in this particular space today in generative AI, if you're trying to, as an LLM player, to do your own chip, you cannot afford to have a chip that is just good enough. You need the best chip(s) that is around because you're competing against other LLM players. And most of all, you're also competing against NVIDIA, who is by no means letting down their guard. They are producing better and better chips with every passing generation.

Later on the Q1 2026 call, CEO Tan says the Tomahawk 6, introduced nine months before the call, runs at 200G SerDes, and they are the only company doing DSP at 1.6 terabit:

And our customers and the hyperscalers want to run with the best networking and with the most bandwidth out there for their clusters. So we are seeing huge demand for this only 100 terabit per second switch out there. So that's driving a lot of demand. And couple that with running bandwidth on scaling out optical transceivers at 1.6 terabit. We are again the only player out there doing DSP at 1.6 terabit.

At speeds near 200G, signals degrade to the point where DSP is not an enhancement - it is what makes communication possible. As AI clusters scale, the bottleneck is increasingly interconnect bandwidth and power efficiency rather than raw compute. This shifts value toward technologies like SerDes and DSP, which enable chips to communicate reliably at extreme speeds. These considerations mean that even if customers pursue COT, one of the hardest parts of the system to replicate is Broadcom’s SerDes and DSP stack.

Success In An AI World

In the first full quarter since the November 2022 ChatGPT announcement, Broadcom had revenue of $8.7 billion through April 2023. The guidance for the quarter through April 2026 is revenue of $22 billion, which implies a 3-year CAGR of 36%.

Looking at numbers on an annual basis, fiscal 2023 revenue was $35.8 billion, and this grew to $51.6 billion in fiscal 2024 and $63.9 billion in fiscal 2025. In the Q1 2026 call, CEO Tan said they have line of sight of significantly more than $100 billion in revenue from AI chips alone in fiscal 2027.

They’ll arrive at $100 billion in overall revenue before they hit the above mark of $100 billion from AI chips alone. This should happen very soon, seeing as the revenue for the most recent quarter ending February 1 came to $19.3 billion, which gives us an annual run rate of $77.2 billion. The guidance for the next quarter is $22 billion, which gives us an annual run rate of $88 billion, and I won’t be surprised if they hit an annual run rate of $100 billion in the quarter after next.

Valuation

One of the valuation risks is overinvestment in the AI space. We know capital doesn’t have a history of going to new markets evenly. Sooner or later it is highly likely we will get over our skis in this area, and this will likely hit all the companies in the space, including Broadcom.

Despite Broadcom’s advantages with SerDes and DSP, there are still valuation risks of customers pivoting and taking business out of the ecosystem. One example of this is the way Google has been turning to MediaTek (MDTK.F) for lower-end inference TPUs. Forward-looking investors need to keep tabs on this to see if Google’s pivot stays limited to low-end inference or if it starts expanding to high-end inference and training.

High stock-based compensation (“SBC”) can be a valuation risk. Even if Broadcom’s technical moat is durable, true value creation depends on how much of the advantage goes to shareholders on a per-share level. Broadcom shows SBC costs and expenses of $7.6 billion hitting their income statement for the fiscal year through November 2, 2025, and $479 million is related to equity awards assumed in connection with the VMware merger. The economic impact of SBC is higher than this GAAP figure, and it is a hidden risk because the per-share earning power is less than what we see on the GAAP accrual income statement. My economic estimate for SBC considers (1) the cost of stopping net dilution by buying back shares, (2) the cost of limiting gross dilution by absorbing some shares by paying taxes, (3) the offset from SBC proceeds, and (4) the offset from SBC tax benefits.

The statement of equity shows gross issuance of 88 million shares for the last fiscal year, and most of these were for SBC. However, the net figure is 71 million because 17 million were withheld. Per the income statement, 16 million shares were repurchased for $2,450 million, but the cost of buying back all 71 million would have been 4.4375x higher, or $10.9 billion, for #1 above. Consideration #2, the withholding of the 17 million shares, is $3.7 billion per the statement of equity. The cash flow statement shows $0.2 billion for #3 above. The pre-tax income was $22.729 billion, and the reconciliation of the effective tax rate table shows a line for excess tax benefits from SBC of 9.6%, which comes to about $2.2 billion for #4 above. Adding these together, my economic SBC impact estimate is $12.2 billion, or $10.9 billion + $3.7 billion - $0.2 billion - $2.2 billion. As a sanity check, the fiscal 2025 10-K says 87 million RSUs vested in the year with a cumulative fair value of $21.7 billion, implying a price of $249.42 each against a grant date fair value of $71.63 each. This is a risk because a company’s valuation is based on the amount of cash that can be pulled out of it from now until it goes out of business on a per-share basis. The FCF figures provided by management don’t subtract the GAAP SBC or economic SBC, but investors need to think about this when contemplating economic FCF figures. The larger the SBC impact, the more of a hit on a per-share basis.

Here are some of the numbers I think about for valuation. Again, the FCF figures below do not treat SBC like cash, so they are overstated relative to the economic FCF ($ in billions):

Again, they’ll have to get to $100 billion in overall revenue fairly soon before they reach the mark of $100 billion from AI chips alone. Looking at the quarters above, the average FCF margin based on the FCF figures provided by management has been around 43%, which means we should see annual FCF of around $43 billion soon when they get to $100 billion in overall revenue. Given the prodigious growth we’re seeing, I think a multiple on this of 30 to 35x is reasonable, implying a valuation range of $1.29 to $1.51 trillion. As I said in one of the paragraphs discussing valuation risks above, the adjusted FCF where SBC is treated as cash is lower than the FCF figures from management above. This is one of the reasons why my valuation multiple is not higher.

The Q1 2026 10-Q shows 4,734,668,184 shares as of February 27, 2026. Multiplying by the March 25 share price of $318.81 gives us a market cap of about $1.51 trillion. Even though Broadcom is doing a tremendous job participating in the new AI world, the stock already has this priced in. The stock is near the high end of my valuation range, and it appears to reflect many of the SerDes and DSP advantages, making it more of a hold than a clear buying opportunity. I think the stock is a hold for long-term investors who plan to own it for a minimum of three years.

data.title: QuantumScape's Defense Angle: Why A Board Appointment Could Matter More Than It Looks
data.description:
QuantumScape’s (QS) recent board appointment may prove more important than the headline suggests.

According to the company's March 5 announcement, QuantumScape added Ross Niebergall to its board, an executive with deep ties across the defense industrial base, including senior roles at L3Harris (LHX), Raytheon (RTX), ThalesRaytheonSystems, and Aerojet Rocketdyne. On its face, that looks like a governance update. But I suspect the move may be better understood as a strategic signal: QuantumScape could be laying groundwork for a path into defense-adjacent energy systems.

That would matter, not because defense suddenly becomes the company’s core business, but because it could accelerate the exact part of the QuantumScape story that the market still doubts most: industrial transfer.

The story has changed

QuantumScape is no longer mainly a battery science story. It is becoming a battery IP, process, and technology transfer story.

The company is now unusually explicit about how it wants to make money. In its February 2026 investor presentation, QuantumScape lays out two monetization paths: near-term collaboration and development work that generates customer cash, and longer-term “high-touch licensing” with recurring royalties, upfront license fees, or prepaid royalties. That is a major shift from the old assumption that QS would need to become a capital-heavy battery manufacturer in its own right.

The PowerCo relationship is the clearest proof of that pivot. The deck describes a non-exclusive license covering up to 85 GWh, including a $130 million royalty prepayment and over $150 million of conditional cash inflows when realized customer billings are included. In other words, QuantumScape is trying to monetize both the industrialization process and the downstream production ramp.

That is the business model the market is being asked to underwrite.

The problem is that investors still need evidence that the model can travel. QuantumScape itself is very clear that the key remaining risks are quality, consistency, reliability, cost, throughput, scaling Eagle Line, and getting the technology platform ready for transfer to prospective licensees. The chemistry is increasingly credible. The commercial structure is improving. The bottleneck is now manufacturing transfer.

That is where defense becomes interesting.

Why defense could matter

Automotive remains the core market, and it should. But auto is also the hardest commercialization path in many ways: volumes are huge, price pressure is intense, qualification cycles are long, and customers demand industrial perfection before meaningful scale.

Defense is different.

A defense customer often cares less about having the cheapest battery on the global cost curve and more about having the best battery for the mission. That means the value stack changes. Energy density matters more. Weight matters more. Fast charging matters more. Safety matters more. Secure domestic supply matters more. The willingness to pay for differentiated performance is also often higher.

QuantumScape’s technology maps reasonably well to that demand profile. The company says its anode-free architecture can improve energy density, enable roughly 15 minute fast charging, offer a nonflammable and noncombustible separator, eliminate anode host material and related manufacturing costs, and reduce dependence on China-dominated graphite supply chains. QSE-5, which QS now calls its first commercial product, is presented as a roughly 5 Ah cell with 844 Wh/L and sub-15-minute 10%–80% charging, with a measured 12.2-minute result at 45°C in B-sample testing.

That does not prove defense adoption. But it does explain why the technology could be attractive in defense-adjacent applications such as unmanned flight systems, loitering munitions, robotics, high-performance portable power, or other systems where battery performance is a mission enabler rather than a commodity input.

The real value is not “selling to defense.” It is proving transfer

This is the part I think the market could be missing.

The real strategic benefit of a defense move would not necessarily be near-term revenue. It would be an acceleration of QuantumScape’s industrial transfer thesis.

If QuantumScape signs a defense-aligned partnership with a prime contractor or a domestic manufacturing partner, the likely form would not be “QS becomes a defense prime.” It would be something closer to this:

QuantumScape provides the technology, process IP, and production blueprint. The partner provides the secure manufacturing environment, systems integration, and access to end applications.

That would fit almost perfectly with the company’s stated model. QuantumScape wants to monetize development work up front, then license the platform for downstream scale. A defense-aligned licensing relationship would validate exactly that.

This is why a defense angle could be an accelerant.

First, it could accelerate process packaging.

A serious defense customer cannot rely on vague PowerPoint engineering. It needs a process that is documented, repeatable, and secure. That would force QuantumScape to turn Eagle Line from an internal pilot effort into a true transfer package. The company itself frames Eagle Line as “forming blueprint for production of QS technology”. Defense could become one of the first arenas where that blueprint gets pressure-tested in the real world.

Second, it could accelerate partner validation.

QuantumScape’s February deck emphasizes building a global ecosystem and operationalizing a capital-light business model. A defense prime inside that ecosystem would be meaningful external validation that QS is moving beyond battery R&D into technology transfer.

Third, it could accelerate economic credibility.

Defense-adjacent markets often tolerate lower volumes and higher unit economics than auto. That makes them potentially ideal as bridge markets. If the company can prove the process in a smaller, high-value environment, it may not need full EV scale economics on day one to convince investors that the licensing model is real.

That is the essence of the idea: defense is not the final market that justifies the entire equity story, but it could be the market that helps prove the business model.

Why the board appointment matters

This is where the Niebergall appointment becomes strategically relevant.

A board member with decades of experience across major defense primes does not guarantee contracts. But it can matter in much more practical ways than investors sometimes appreciate. It can help with introductions. It can help the company understand how to frame its technology for defense use cases. It can help navigate qualification pathways, domestic manufacturing expectations, and secure supply-chain priorities. It can help QuantumScape speak the language of defense procurement and prime contractor ecosystems rather than the language of pure venture-backed battery innovation.

That is valuable if QuantumScape wants to become not just a battery company but a platform company with strategic applications.

The right way to think about the opportunity

I do not think investors should jump to the conclusion that QuantumScape is about to become a major defense supplier. That would be far too aggressive.

The better way to think about it is this:

Automotive remains the main long-term market. But defense-adjacent applications could become a high-value proving ground for the company’s technology transfer model.

That matters because QuantumScape’s biggest unanswered question is no longer whether it can make a compelling cell. The company has made progress there. It matters more that it has a clearer first product in QSE-5, improving separator throughput claims through Raptor and Cobra, and a more explicit capital-light licensing strategy than it did even a year ago.

The real question now is whether those ingredients can be turned into a repeatable industrial system that outside partners can trust and scale.

A defense relationship could help answer that question faster than the market expects.

The risk

There is, of course, a risk that investors over-romanticize the defense angle.

Defense markets can be slow, bespoke, and difficult to scale. Program wins can sound more important than they are. A company can spend years talking about “dual use” applications and still generate immaterial revenue.

That is why I would not treat defense as a replacement for the core thesis. I would treat it as a possible accelerant to the core thesis.

If QuantumScape remains stuck in what I would call pilot-line purgatory, where it has promising demos and interesting partners but not enough throughput, quality, or transfer readiness, then defense will not save the story.

Bottom line

The most interesting defense bull case for QuantumScape is not that it becomes a weapons supplier. It is that defense becomes the first market where its manufacturing blueprint gets licensed, trusted, and fielded.

If that happens, the value is bigger than any one defense program. It would suggest QuantumScape is becoming what it claims it wants to be: a capital-light battery IP and process platform, capable of monetizing both technology development and industrial transfer.

That is why the Niebergall appointment is worth watching. It may turn out to be more than a board refresh. It may be an early sign that QuantumScape is trying to use defense as a bridge from promising battery chemistry to trusted manufacturing relevance.

And if that bridge starts to form, the market may begin to revalue the company accordingly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Lots Still Going On
data.description:
The Federal Reserve returned to a policy of quantitative easing around December 1, 2025.

Since then, the amount of securities held outright has increased.

Here is the picture of the securities portfolio since the last Wednesday in November.

The securities portfolio has increased by $126.9 billion since then.

The "war" with Iran began on Saturday, February 28, 2026.

The Fed's portfolio of securities has increased by $39.7 billion since February 25, 2026, the last Wednesday reporting date before February 28.

Looking at these increases in another way, in the "first" part of the quantitative easing, the portfolio increased, on average, $6.4 billion per week.

In the "second" part of the quantitative easing, the part related to the time the U.S. was at "war" with Iran, the portfolio increased, on average, $9.9 billion per week.

So, there was a modest increase in the weekly acquisition of securities for the Fed's securities portfolio in the weeks following the start of the Iran adventure.

The interest here has to do with the speed at which the Fed is adding securities to its portfolio. If the Fed has stepped up the speed at which it is adding securities to its portfolio, one could argue that the "war" was resulting in this faster accumulation of securities. This "increase" could be associated with the government's effort to cause the Fed to accelerate the increase in the securities portfolio, something that President Trump had been hoping Fed Chairman Jerome Powell would do.

The "war" effort could be achieving Trump's goal.

Trump had expressed hope that if the Fed injected more reserves into the banking system, the Fed's policy rate of interest could be reduced to a much lower level.

Even with the added reserves coming into the banking system, market conditions do not seem ready for further reductions in the Fed's policy rate of interest.

Unfortunately, given current market conditions, it appears as if more market participants have started suggesting that maybe the policy rate of interest should be raised...rather than lowered.

Here is the yield on the 2-year U.S. Treasury.

And here is the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury.

The market pressure seems to be "up" for both yields.

Not an easy case for a reduction in yields!

The economy seems to be well supplied with money.

The year-over-year rate of growth of the M2 money stock is presently 4.4 percent. The economy seems to have all the money it needs to continue to support U.S. economic growth in the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent range.

The major concern that still seems to be bothering lots of people is the possibility that inflation could get out-of-control over the next year or two.

With the M2 money stock growing at only a 4.4 percent year-over-year rate of increase and the real economy growing at 2.0 percent or more, excessive inflation does not seem to be a sure thing.

But, given other things that are going on in government...like tariffs...and like "war"...rising levels of inflation do not seem to be out of the realm of possibility.

Furthermore, there is the environment in which a new Federal Reserve chairman might be in the near future.

Mr. Powell's term as Federal Reserve chairman may be coming to an end...but...Mr. Powell may not be leaving the Federal Reserve Board.

Even though his tenure as Chairman may be over...his term as a board member is not over until 2028. It seems as if we hear more and more about this as we move down the calendar.

So...stay tuned.

data.title: Pfizer: Why Wall Street Habitually Overestimates Target Price
data.description:
Pfizer stock: Wall Street loves it again

I last covered Pfizer Inc. stock (NYSE:PFE) on Feb 19. The article rated the stock as hold after examining its FQ4 earnings report (ER) and dividend declarations. Since then, a few new catalysts have caught my attention and motivated this follow-up. The rest of this article will focus on three of them: the sentiment changes from Wall Street, the new development for the “TrumpRx" and "Most-Favored-Nation" pricing models, and also the updates on its pipeline.

Let me start with the sentiment change. As shown in the chart below, Wall Street analysts, after downgrading its rating to hold briefly in Feb 2025, fell back in love with stock again. To wit, Pfizer currently receives an average rating of 3.5 from them, leading to a buy rating. Out of the 28 analysts who rated the stock, 10 of them issued a strong buy or a buy rating. Only 3 of them gave the stock a rating of sell or lower.

With this backdrop, next I will argue why I disagree with the current Wall Street ratings. I believe the buy rating is overly optimistic and ignores the various earnings and growth headwinds.

PFE stock: Wall Street habitually overestimates

Broadening the horizon a bit, Wall Street analysts have systematically overrated the stock and also overestimated its price target in the past few years. As an example, the chart below compares their price target (the orange line) to the actual share prices (the blue line) in the past 5 years. As seen, despite the continued downward revisions of their price target in 2022, their estimates still turned out to be overly optimistic price targets. Since 2024, the average analyst target price remained stubbornly around $30, while the stock price plummeted to as low as $22 and remains under the price target despite recent price recoveries.

Looking ahead, I see several headwinds for PFE share price to remain under pressure. The strongest one in my view must be the political uncertainties involving future drug pricing. As a background, the Trump administration has introduced the so-called “TrumpRx" and "Most-Favored-Nation" drug pricing models. These models are expected to cause margin compression and are the key driver for PFE’s valuation compression in the recent 1~2 years. But, unlike the many bullish analysts, I don’t believe the headwinds have run their course yet. Judging by the latest developments (see an example quoted below), the expected savings from these new drug pricing model may not have been fully realized and more potential impacts could unfold.

Mintz News (March 20): The first week of March 2026 brought coordinated congressional oversight targeting the Trump administration's most-favored-nation (MFN) drug pricing initiative. Congressional leaders sent several letters to President Trump and pharmaceutical manufacturers requesting details about the MFN agreements such manufacturers struck with the administration….

Although each MFN agreement was publicly announced, the exact details of these agreements remain largely confidential. Neither the administration nor the participating manufacturers have shared the agreements with Congress. Moreover, many of the MFN agreements contain confidentiality provisions that prohibit the manufacturers from publicly disclosing the agreements’ terms. As a result, the scope and practical impact of the MFN pricing deals cannot be independently evaluated.

PFE stock: valuation is not as attractive as it seems

One could certainly make the argument that the above uncertainties are already baked into Pfizer’s current P/E ratios. Indeed, the stock is trading at a FWD P/E of only 9.3x as you can see from the following chart, which does seem like a bargain either by horizontal or vertical comparison.

But I beg to differ. In my assessment, the current P/E is not cheap enough to fully capture the growth and earnings uncertainties. As an example, the stock is expected to suffer earnings declines for the next 4 years. Given PFE’s status as a mature player in the staple healthcare sector, I believe the so-called Graham’s P/E (as detailed below) is a reasonable approach to assess its growth-adjusted valuation.

The formula described by Graham originally in the 1962 edition of Security Analysis is as follows: P/E = 8.5 + 2*g, where g is a greasonably expected growth rate for the next few years.

Following this approach, a stagnating business would be worth 8.5x P/E. PFE’s current P/E of 9.3x is about 10% more expensive; and moreover, its EPS is likely to not only stagnate but also decline in the years to come.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the policy uncertainties, a few other downside risks are also worth mentioning. The company is facing upcoming LOE (loss of exclusivity) for several of its franchises and continued pressure on its COVID-19 business, which has been drastically declining since the pandemic broke out.

There are certainly positives, and I am rating the stock as a hold after all. To start, PFE’s current dividend yield is about 6.27%. As shown in the next chart, this is well above its historical average (and also the sector average). Furthermore, the current annual payout of $1.72 per share is well covered by its EPS. As shown in the chart above, despite the expected earnings decline, the EPS is expected to be in the range of $2.17 to $2.96 in the next few years, resulting in a payout ratio in the range of 58% to 79% at the current payout. Such a lucrative and well-covered yield should provide a good amount of downside protection and also a support for the share prices.

Finally, the company has also reported a series of progress to the development of its pipeline drugs, adding some clarity to the replacement plan for the upcoming LOE and also the decline of COVID-19 drugs. A notable example involves its Talzenna and Xtandi, which just reported encourage results in a late-stage trial (see more details quoted below). Another pipeline drug, , atirmociclib that targets breast cancer, also posted encourage results in a mid-stage trial.

Seeking Alpha News: Pfizer (PFE) announced on Thursday that its cancer drug regimen, Talzenna plus Xtandi, met the primary endpoint in a Phase 3 trial for a new indication in prostate cancer. Citing topline data, the New York-based pharma giant said that TALAPRO-3 met the primary goal, as patients on Talzenna plus Xtandi experienced a statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in radiographic progression-free survival compared to those on placebo plus Xtandi.

To close, this PFE follow-up mostly focused on the recent sentiment change on Wall Street, the development of the drug pricing models, and also the updates of its pipeline drugs. After examining these developments, my verdict is that Wall Street’s current rating and price targets are overly optimistic. I keep seeing a neutral risk profile, reflecting a balance of conflicting forces underlying the stock.

data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: Deeply Undervalued As Cannabis Rescheduling Hits Milestone
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I last covered Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR), I highlighted this REIT's excellent balance sheet and significant discount, with the AFFO feeling some more tenant pressure that could threaten the double-digit dividend.

Following a solid quarter and some improvements seen so far in Q1, IIPR remains a Strong Buy, with the stock still trading at a significant discount despite very important developments in the industry seen not long ago.

Internal Developments

IIPR reported a strong Q4, beating the market’s FFO and revenue estimates and delivering an increase in AFFO compared to previous quarters, while 2025’s AFFO fell to $205.41 million.

They also announced some improvements regarding rent collection from tenants, with $3.74 million from the defaulted tenant Gold Flora and $0.24 million from PharmaCann, with some improvements seen so far in 2026 while the company is diversifying, with the IQHQ deal mentioned previously also adding interest payments going forward, with Q4 benefitting from $5.0 million worth of interest from it.

A couple of weeks ago, IIPR also announced significant updates, executing on 122,000 square feet of new leases in March 2026 while resolving all pending litigation with PharmaCann for their prior lease defaults and actively working on finding new tenants for their properties. Meanwhile, they announced leasing the properties previously held by Gold Flora, reaching some tentative agreements for the four assets leased to 4Front, while The Cannabist Company defaulted on one Pennsylvania property that represents 2.7% of their total rental revenues (out of 20 leased to them - 6.3% of total rent), similar to Battle Green.

Financially, based on IIPR’s latest report, we continue to see a very strong balance sheet, with some of the lowest levels of leverage in the entire REIT sector, although they have $291.2 million worth of their 5.50% unsecured senior notes are due in May 2026 that will have to be refinanced/dealt with very, very soon, potentially adding some more interest expenses given the current environment, although it’s still not that significant given its size and they also have a new $100 million revolving credit facility with a 6.1% interest rate.

Common Versus Preferred

IIPR announced another $1.90 quarterly dividend not long ago, bringing their annualized yield to a whopping 14.6% today, representing about $215 million based on the current number of shares, for a payout ratio of ~105%, which may be an issue despite having enough cash to cover it. Annualizing their Q4 AFFO, however, which includes the IQHQ payments, we’re talking about $213.33 million, and while potential tenant improvements can boost this into sustainability, I wouldn’t mind a cut.

Also, they recently announced a new $100 million buyback program after repurchasing only ~$20.11 million last year.

The company also has an interesting cumulative preferred stock (IIPR.PR.A), which currently pays a yield of ~10% compared to its 9% base yield, with a liquidation preference of $25 compared to the current price of $22.47, redeemable starting on October 19, 2022, and very well covered by their income.

Not only is this one theoretically very safe from redeeming (despite being past its date) since the common offers a ~45% higher yield and that one should be a priority over redeeming, but the company is actively issuing more of this preferred stock, raising $40 million in 2026 already thanks to it, with the Executive Chairman mentioning the following during the Q4 Earnings Call:

We also strengthened our liquidity position for the year by raising $100 million under a new revolving credit facility in October and issuing approximately $25 million of preferred stock through our ATM. For 2026, we continue to access the capital markets opportunistically and have raised over $40 million of preferred stock at an attractive yield of just over 9.5%, surpassing the amount we raised in all of 2025.

Given the fact that the common stock comes with a 45% better yield and a significant discount to fair value, I wouldn’t pick the preferred over it, as the discrepancy is way too big to ignore now. Even if they cut the yield of the common (which is not necessarily needed right now, but I also wouldn’t mind it), this would likely still be above the preferred, but I don’t mean to discredit the latter, as getting a 10% preferred yield from a healthy and nearly debt-free company that can benefit from significant long-term tailwinds is still not something we see every day, but the risk-reward of the common fits my portfolio better.

Major Macro Developments

As mentioned in the recent NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) analysis, by far the biggest development for the industry has been the long-awaited rescheduling following Executive Order 14370, which expedites the process of moving marijuana from Schedule I to Schedule III, being a major milestone towards the industry’s future. This can have very significant benefits for many aspects of the industry, including taxes, deductions, etc., which NLCP even said before could sum up to over $400 million in annual savings.

And again, that’s on top of the hemp crackdown that can support their tenants given the unfair advantages it had, which NLCP also mentioned as a potential catalyst that can help stabilize retail pricing and support their growth. The company’s CEO also talked about this during their Q4 Earnings Call:

If enacted, rescheduling may eliminate the punitive impact of 280E for our tenants, which we believe would meaningfully improve operator cash flows, strengthen credit profiles and support additional investments across the industry. In addition, the executive order highlighted concerns regarding the proliferation of hemp-derived THC products. Recent legislation closing certain loopholes under the 2018 Farm Bill is expected to restrict hemp-derived THC products beginning in November 2026, which should reduce unregulated products and support consumer safety across the U.S. At the state level, we are tracking several meaningful catalysts on the horizon, including the potential commencement of adult-use sales in Virginia and possible adult-use legalization in Pennsylvania and Florida.

However, even though these companies have very long-term lease terms (12.8-year weighted average lease term for IIPR), I believe it’s fair and safe to assume that their cap rates will gradually fall. This rescheduling also makes it easier for their tenants to become more “creditworthy,” potentially leading to a fall in cap rates as they would have other options to borrow money at more attractive yields, although this doesn’t mean that IIPR’s AFFO would drop as they can benefit from an increase in volume as competition would rise alongside the industry, although this rescheduling won't magically fix everything overnight.

Somewhat of a deterrent or a slowdown could be the ongoing Iran conflict, which has clearly visible effects on pump prices from day one and could lead to longer-term issues, although I mentioned before that I don’t see this as something that would last for too long given its implications on the global economy, but it’s important to keep in mind that a recession/slowdown is terrible for a company like this, especially since their tenants already had issues even before.

Another risk from this stems from the effects it can have on inflation, with the Fed recently delaying rate cuts and warning about the potential shock caused by this conflict, while the ECB announced very recently that it’s ready to hike rates even if the inflation surge is short-lived, which can be terrible for IIPR and its tenants, especially since we’re talking about a REIT, as mentioned previously.

Keep in mind that rates were hiked to fight inflation, as that’s ultimately their role, even though it comes with a natural slowdown of the economy and an increase in borrowing costs, while US Treasury yields already jumped >10% (from below 4% at the end of February 2026 to over 4.46% on March 27; 10-year notes) as a result of the strengthening dollar, as the market retreated into it. This can hurt the country even more, as these attractive yields can attract even more dollars and increase borrowing costs, which can be reflected in the SOFR as well.

However, there’s also the scenario where a potential reversal in the recent interest for US dollars and high uncertainty due to fuel prices trading at a war-related premium could help IIPR’s valuation, which has been under some pressure since the conflict escalated.

Still, the long-term potential, especially following this rescheduling, remains significant, as we’re talking about an industry that can only now start to shine, with a 7% CAGR expected from 2024 to 2029, similar to the growth seen in R&D spending in life science, where IIPR recently expanded through the deal with IQHQ (which is co-founded by IIPR's Executive Chairman and Co-Founder, as a note) mentioned before, getting a 15% yield preferred stock and a 13.5% initial yield floating loan.

Risks Reiterated

Of course, you don’t get this type of yield without risk at a time when traditional REITs get 6-8% cap rates, and that should be clear for everyone when understanding the type of investment we’re talking about.

Another very particular risk of this industry is the tenant dependency (which is not the worst out there, I’ll add). Given the low number of possible clients (although the rescheduling should help bring more competition), combined with their very specialized properties, it can make it very tough to find new tenants in case a tenant goes bankrupt. We’re not talking about retail properties in high-quality Sun Belt areas, and it’s important to value that properly.

Valuation

For IIPR's DCF to Equity, annualizing their Q4 2025 AFFO to take into account the IQHQ interest and potential tenant improvements, I'll start with $215 million in AFFO in 2026.

Given the industry’s potential growth and the company’s room to expand, especially if the yield falls (either through a cut or the stock rising), let’s assume a 6% CAGR until 2030, given potential tenant improvements or replacements on top, followed by a 4% CAGR until 2035 and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a high 14% discount rate to take into account the risks and demand a solid margin of safety, with 28.30 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.15 billion, for an intrinsic value of $76.09 per share, which is still above the current $52.09, while less conservative estimates can easily get us over $100, assuming that the industry would get closer to maturity and stabilize. As a note, if we divide the terminal value by the year 10 AFFO, we'd be at a P/AFFO of about 8.9x, which I really don't think is much to ask for, especially if they prove their potential.

Conclusions

IIPR remains a Strong Buy, backed by a significant discount, strong double-digit dividend yield, and impressive long-term potential to grow alongside its industry, which got significant wins recently.

There are definitely risks to keep in mind, but even if we demand a high margin of safety through the discount rate, we’re still well above the current levels, while the company’s financial position is among the best we can find (NLCP’s is better since they have virtually zero debt), offering significant levels of flexibility.

data.title: Brown-Forman: A Deal With Pernod Ricard Could Be A Game Changer
data.description:
The industry-wide headwinds within the spirits sector have decimated all major players within the industry and are now spurring deals that a couple of years ago would have seemed unlikely.

Discussions about a potential acquisition of Brown-Forman (BF.B) by Pernod Ricard (PRNDY) are now being held, and this could create a truly global brand powerhouse in the sector that will rival the current leader, Diageo (DEO).

All three names mentioned above are down by more than 60% over the past 5 years, which shows the severity of the current downturn that has likely facilitated the ongoing talks.

Additionally, the fact that both BF.B and PRNDY are down significantly over that period, and trading at relatively comparable multiples means that a share-based acquisition would not hurt BF.B investors when it comes to their future share within the combined entity.

Therefore, the more important questions right now are how accretive a potential deal would be for both companies and what the potential benefits and risks involved are.

A Major Rival Of Diageo

Right off the bat, a potential acquisition would allow the combined entity to rival the current hegemon in the sector—Diageo. In terms of revenues, it would still be smaller than DEO, but in a much better position to take advantage of economies of scale.

In terms of valuation multiples, BF.B now trades at a notable premium to both DEO and PRNDY. The difference is noticeable both on EV/EBITDA (see the graph above) and on a forward price/earnings basis (see the graph below).

While this is good news for BF.B shareholders, it should be noted that the company was trading at significantly higher multiples relative to PRNDY just a couple of years ago. That means that Brown-Forman's management is obliged to negotiate a higher target price for the company.

Synergies

The word synergies is often used left and right when a potential acquisition or a merger is being discussed. Quite often, however, promises of cost savings turn sour for shareholders, especially in industries with a high relative share of intangible assets.

As a heavily branded sector, the spirits industry is definitely a good example of an area where tangible assets are of relatively low importance and potential synergies would be questionable.

Nonetheless, a combination between BF.B and PRNDY would be very attractive from a synergy point of view for a number of reasons.

First, both companies have selling, general & administrative expenses to sales ratios of around 32% to 33%. The share of fixed costs to sales has been gradually falling over the years as companies grew, but this process has been very slow. On the other hand, Diageo, as we saw above, is much larger in terms of revenues, and has an SG&A-to-sales ratio of 18%.

This gap between BF.B and PRNDY on one hand and DEO on the other show the ballpark figure of potential economies of scale and cost savings if post-acquisition restructuring is done right. Of course, investors should be mindful of the fact that this is a tedious process that is usually associated with some increased up-front restructuring costs.

The case for realizing these enormous synergies is strengthened further by the geographical exposure of the two companies. Brown-Forman, for example, generates roughly half of its sales from the United States, and its Jack Daniel's brand is one of the most well-known in the country. Woodford Reserve, on the other hand, is well-positioned in the premium segment.

The space occupied by Jack Daniel's and Woodford Reserve is an area where PRDNY is weak when it comes to its large strategic international brands. Here PRNDY has iconic vodka, gin, rum, Scotch, and Irish whiskey brands, but it lacks an American whiskey brand that is comparable to those owned by Brown-Forman.

Pernod Ricard would also benefit from Brown-Forman's distribution network in the United States, where it was lagging and struggling to close the gap with peers.

The ready-to-drink category is also an area where the two companies would be complementary to each other, as Brown-Forman had huge success with its Jack Daniel's Ready-to-Drink, while PRDNY has been gearing for growth with its non-alcoholic and RTD offerings.

Overall, the two companies are an almost perfect fit when it comes to geographical and product segmentation, which, in my view, significantly strengthens the case for notable synergies to be realized.

One potential issue that investors would need to be aware of is the cultural aspect of the two businesses. As an American family-controlled entity, Brown-Forman differs from PRDNY, which is a French company that is structured around the founding Ricard family. While the cultural aspect is very subjective to analyze, and I would abstain from making any generalizations in that regard, investors should be aware that a potential combination would not be as smooth as merging two entities from the country.

Investor Takeaway

In a nutshell, it is difficult to argue against a possible combination between BF.B and PRNDY. The two companies are an almost perfect fit when it comes to geographical and product segmentation. It is still too early to draw any solid conclusions about a potential deal, and more information is needed, but a combined entity could be a serious threat for one of my personal favorites in the sector—Diageo. As more details about the acquisition emerge, investors would need to be mindful of the target price that would be put on BF.B. as it used to trade at even higher multiples to PRNDY. Lastly, the cultural aspect of the two companies should not be ignored, as it could easily create some bumps down the road.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia Won't Be Dead Money For Much Longer
data.description:
Near the end of last year, I upgraded Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) to a Strong Buy rating for the first time. Citing fundamental strengthening, solid guidance, and valuation contraction, I believed the stock was in a strong position to deliver outperformance.

So far, that hasn't played out. Since the publication of that piece, Nvidia has actually seen modest declines, and overall, the stock has really been dead money for around half a year now. With their latest results having come out in late February and the GTC happening not long ago, I have decided to provide a much-needed update for readers.

Below, it is shown that Nvidia's fundamentals have continued to strengthen. Despite some risks moving forward, solid guidance and new opportunities show that the long-term prospects remain bright. Meanwhile, the valuation has seen steep contraction, and the forward P/E is now at a discount to the sector. Nvidia stock has undeniably been dead money for an extended period, but given the current setup, I would say it is likely to wake up sooner rather than later. Therefore, I'm reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Growth Rebound Continues

We'll start off today with an overview of their latest numbers to see how their financial performance has been recently. For FY2026 Q4, Nvidia generated record revenues of $68.1 billion, up an impressive 73.21% YoY. As you can see in the chart above, top-line growth has now accelerated for a second straight quarter to confirm that an inflection point has indeed happened. CEO Jensen Huang stated that

Computing demand is growing exponentially — the agentic AI inflection point has arrived. Grace Blackwell with NVLink is the king of inference today — delivering an order-of-magnitude lower cost per token — and Vera Rubin will extend that leadership even further.

In my view, the considerable rebound in growth after an extended period of deceleration shows that Huang's observations are correct and that this robust demand is translating directly into financial growth for the company. Just recently, there have been further signals that demand will continue to be solid. Earlier this month, Elon Musk stated that SpaceX (SPACE), xAI (X.AI), and Tesla (TSLA) will keep purchasing Nvidia chips at scale, and Amazon (AMZN) AWS also committed to buying another 1 million chips by the end of 2027.

Lastly, it should be noted that Nvidia beat analyst expectations by $1.9 billion to extend their streak of outperformance. All in all, I would say that top-line results show business activity continues to be extraordinarily strong for the company, as demand has no signs of softening at this point.

Don't Overlook Networking

For their all-important data center business, results were impressive given how large their growth base is. They reported total revenues in this segment of $62.3 billion, up 75% YoY to outpace the company-wide top-line growth rate. This shows that data center solutions continue to be a growth leader for Nvidia. That said, while state-of-the-art GPUs like Blackwell Ultra get all the buzz in the media, the growth we have seen in networking is exceptional.

As you can see above, compute revenues grew by a very respectable 57%, but note that networking revenues soared by 267%. While compute is, of course, still the much larger business, investors shouldn't overlook the potential and importance of their networking solutions. In their earnings presentation, they stated that they now have the "world’s largest networking business" and given their growth rate, I would say they are in a position to extend their lead. Overall, I believe it is clear that their key data center segment continues to see very robust momentum.

Profitability Improves

In addition to rebounding revenue growth, profitability continues to move in the right direction despite earlier fears that strong margins can't be kept up forever. For gross margin, there were 1.7 percentage points of expansion YoY, and so Nvidia has controlled costs well. They attributed this strength to lower inventory provisions.

As for operating expenses, there was a notable 51% increase YoY due to investments for future growth. However, since this is considerably lower than the revenue growth rate, operating efficiency has actually improved, and operating margin has, of course, expanded as well.

This came through to the bottom line as they saw a nice 82% jump in their EPS. This is an acceleration of growth as well, and they also beat expectations here. As a whole, the profitability of the company is in an increasingly strong position, and of course that is an encouraging sign.

Q1 Guidance

Before talking about some opportunities and risks moving forward, let's take a look at their financial guidance for FY2027 Q1. At the midpoint, Nvidia sees revenues of $78 billion, and that would represent a 77% YoY growth rate. Since a 73% increase was reported in Q4, the company is now on track to see a third consecutive quarter of growth acceleration. This indicates Nvidia is regaining momentum for their business trajectory, and it should be noted that this top-line guidance was also above consensus.

For gross margin, they are projecting 75% at the midpoint. This would be a clear improvement from the year-ago quarter's gross margin (excluding the H2O charge) of 71.3%, and so cost control will remain strong. Lastly, they see around $7.5 billion in operating expenses. That is a bit high, as a 109% jump is implied, but given that they're continuing to invest in the future of the business, I would say that this is not a major concern at this point. In my view, Q1 guidance is solid and suggests continued fundamental strength moving forward.

Opportunities

The first major opportunity moving forward would be the reopening of the Chinese market to their chips. On March 17th, CEO Huang stated that the company had received orders from China and that they were working on restarting manufacturing. Just a day later, there were reports that the Chinese Government had approved the H200 for sale. While there is, of course, competition from domestic companies like Huawei, the reintroduction of their chips in a large market should help to drive growth. Furthermore, plans to sell Groq chips there could further boost their prospects in the country. Just recently, Wells Fargo (WFC) expressed their view that China could add $25 billion or more to Nvidia's annual revenue, and so the positive developments recently are a big deal.

With SpaceX planning to go public later this year to raise capital for space data centers, it is becoming increasingly obvious that there is a growing opportunity in this area. For Nvidia, their quick move to release the Space-1 Vera Rubin Module at GTC 2026 shows that they are adapting to the needs of the market without hesitation. While it is much too early to quantify how big the opportunity and market really are, I would say that this new frontier for data centers offers Nvidia another potential growth driver in the coming years and beyond.

Risks

A first risk that should be noted is their Groq deal. Their agreement was non-exclusive licensing for their inference technology and not an acquisition, but it is still drawing some antitrust concerns. Around a week ago, two senators reportedly wrote to Nvidia to inquire about this deal. Questions included whether the transaction was structured this way to avoid antitrust scrutiny. While there doesn't seem to have been formal investigations, I would say that their Groq deal may continue to draw some form of unwanted attention moving forward.

The much larger risk that Nvidia has to deal with is basically competition from their customers. While this isn't breaking news, it should be noted that hyperscalers are continuing to develop their own chips to reduce reliance on Nvidia. Earlier this month, Wedbush Securities stated that Google (GOOGL), Amazon, and Meta (META) are all "aggressively focused" on building alternatives, and so over time, Nvidia's chips may see an erosion in overall market share. The versatility of GPUs like Blackwell Ultra means that there will always be a large market for these chips, but the continued rise of ASICs could nonetheless lower their growth trajectory over time.

Valuation Is Very Cheap

As you can see in the above valuation chart, Nvidia's forward P/E ratio has contracted to a reading of just 20.41. The recent sharp drop is obviously due to a revision of growth targets by analysts, but in my view, the risk/reward is now extremely attractive for the bulls.

In today's article, it was shown that Nvidia's fundamentals have continued to strengthen. Accelerating growth and expanding margin are a dream combo, and recall that guidance for FY2027 Q1 was also solid. Just above, it was shown that there are both opportunities and risks for Nvidia, but given the momentum they are seeing now, I would say that the former outweighs the latter at this point.

With the long-term picture in many ways stronger than it was just a few months ago, I would say that the forward P/E's currently depressed level shows that the market is underappreciating what Nvidia brings to the table. Funny enough, their non-GAAP forward P/E is now at a discount to the IT sector, according to Seeking Alpha, and so the stock is indeed very cheap at current levels.

Conclusion

Nvidia stock has been trading essentially sideways now for quite a while. Even though it has been dead money for months, I believe the current setup shows that it could wake up in the not-so-distant future. Strengthening fundamentals, a bright outlook, and a dirt-cheap valuation are key ingredients to stock outperformance, and Nvidia ticks every one of these boxes. The risk/reward has further improved in favor of the bulls since my previous article, and so I have decided to maintain my strong buy rating on the stock at this juncture.

data.title: Maze Therapeutics: MZE829 Presses Forward Despite Stock Price Drop
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Maze Therapeutics (MAZE) with a "Buy" rating. Just a few days ago, the company reported proof-of-concept [POC] data from its phase 2 HORIZON study using its dual-mechanism APOL1 inhibitor MZE829 for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD]. It was noted that 12 patients given this drug over a 12-week period achieved a clinically meaningful reduction in proteinuria of 35.6% as measured by urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio [uACR]. Despite this, the stock price dropped by 37% despite having reported this positive data. It is not clear why the stock price dropped; maybe investors were expecting more, but the company had met the bar it set out, having shown at least a 30% uACR reduction or better. One possible reason is because a competitor by the name of Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) is also in the process of the development of its drug inaxaplin as an APOL1 inhibitor for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD].

More about this below, but Vertex achieved a greater protein reduction for its phase 2a study; however, I don't believe that the trials should be compared because this study was focused on treating a subset of AMKD patients with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. I believe that the stock price trading lower was a huge overreaction and that this stock remains a "Buy" rating. The company even did well in targeting the FSGS subpopulation as well, providing a greater reduction of proteinuria. The truth is that this program is not ending; therefore, the drop doesn't make much sense. As a matter of fact, it already has plans to initiate a pivotal program using MZE829 to treat AMKD patients. The risk is that Vertex is advancing its phase 2/3 AMPLITUDE study using inaxaplin to treat AMKD patients [broad population].

It is expected to release interim data from this study either by the end of 2026 or early 2027 [Vertex Pharmaceuticals Q4 and Full-year 2025 earnings Presentation February 12, 2026, slide #7]. Lastly, there are additional shots on goal with this company. It already saw positive signals in phase 1 human testing for its SLC6A19 inhibitor MZE782 for the treatment of patients with Phenylketonuria [PKU]. With that said, it is initiating two POC studies in 2026 for this program. One phase 2 study is going to attempt to achieve plasma phenylalanine [Phe] reduction in PKU, and then the other phase 2 study is going to look at proteinuria reduction in chronic kidney disease [CKD] patients. With the ability for this company to press forward towards a pivotal program of MZE829 in AMKD patients, plus additional shots on goal with its SLC6A19 inhibitor MZE782 for PKU and CKD, I believe that this stock should be initiated with a "Buy" rating. The reason for not a "Strong Buy" rating yet is because there remains the risk of the Vertex program in place, which could potentially overshadow MZE829, but it remains to be seen.

MZE829 Data Delivers in AMKD And Stock Plunge Is Huge Overblown Reaction

As I noted above, Maze Therapeutics released data from its phase 2 proof-of-concept [POC] HORIZON study. This mid-stage trial used the company's APOL1 inhibitor MZE829 for the treatment of patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD] The beauty of this trial was that it was set as an open-label basket design trial. This allowed the company to recruit a broad group of AMKD patients, including those who were diabetic, non-diabetic, and non-diabetic patients with FSGS. Before going over all of the clinical data released from this HORIZON study, plus any catalysts to come about for investors to keep an eye on, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD] is characterized as a genetic kidney disorder wherein, in order for a person to get this, they must inherit two risk variants [one from each parent]. These two risk variants are G1 and/or G2 of the APOL1 gene. When this gene is functioning as a normal one, it's responsible for producing a protein that helps the person's immune system. In the case of AMKD patients, there are mutations of this gene, and it causes two items to come about:

  • Damage occurs to parts of the kidney responsible for filtering out blood
  • Cell death in the kidney, which leads to inflammation and scarring

One important factor to note is that not everyone that inherits two copies of an APOL1 risk variant ends up having AMKD. It is believed that even if this actually happens, only about between 15% and 20% have a chance of developing kidney disease. In addition, there is something else to mention about this disorder, which is that if one inherits both copies of the APOL1 gene, there is a 10x likelihood of them getting a disorder known as Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis [FSGS]. With this kidney disorder, there is involvement of scarring of the filtering units of the kidney. I brought this up because the company actually had evaluated a subset of these FSGS patients from the phase 2 HORIZON study. AMKD is a subset of Chronic Kidney Disease [CKD] and is observed primarily in people of African ancestry. The reason why is because the mutation is responsible for a factor of protecting from a parasite that causes African sleeping sickness, but the mutation also causes kidney damage in some. Why does there need to be a disease-modifying therapy like MZE829? That's because there are no currently available targeted therapies for these patients for starters. Also, this subset of CKD patients actually progresses in disease faster than those of non-APOL1-mediated kidney disease. The reason why is because their faster disease progression results in them initiating dialysis 10 years earlier than CKD patients. With AMKD patients, they can suffer from high blood pressure, protein in the urine [proteinuria], fatigue, and swelling of the legs. The most important measure being proteinuria, which the company released with its results from the phase 2 HORIZON study.

In order to see whether or not its APOL1 inhibitor would be effective in being able to treat these patients with APOL1-mediated kidney disease [AMKD], it ran the phase 2 HORIZON trial. This mid-stage study recruited up to a total of 15 patients for both safety and tolerability analysis. However, in terms of evaluation of the efficacy outcome using MZE829, only 12 patients were evaluable. Being that this was an open-label basket trial design, it was laid out to recruit the following, which I mentioned above:

  • APOL1-mediated CKD patients with diabetes
  • APOL1-mediated CKD patients without diabetes
  • Severe FSGS without diabetes

The 12 patients in question, across all these subpopulations, were given 250 mg oral MZE829 over a 12-week period. The final outcome was that all patients achieved a 35.6% mean reduction in proteinuria as measured by urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio [uACR]. Now, for the first reason why I believe that the stock dropping on data was overblown. The threshold the company set out was to achieve an average of at least a ≥30% uACR reduction [Maze Therapeutics HORIZON phase 2 topline data Corporate Presentation March 25, 2026, slide #10]. Why is this important? That's because this is the threshold set out in order for these AMKD patients to both achieve a 10-year delay to End-Stage-Kidney-Disease [ESKD] and achieve an eGFR slope improvement as well. The second reason goes to that of what I noted above about Vertex Pharmaceuticals with inaxaplin, which is focused on targeting CKD patients with two APOL1 variants with FSGS. Again, it has the ongoing phase 2/3 AMPLITUDE study to assess the effect of inaxaplin on kidney function and proteinuria in patients with AMKD. This is a risk because if it releases a positive interim analysis for this patient population by either end of 2026 or early 2027, then it will be compared to all other available data to date [including that of HORIZON]. However, the prior data that Vertex released was that of the inaxaplin effect on proteinuria of patients with two APOL1 variants and FSGS. For this subpopulation of patients, the change in baseline of uACR at week 13 was 47.6%. The outcome for the 4 FSGS patients who received MZE829 in terms of mean uACR reduction at week 12 was said to be that of 61.8%.

The point here is that it is a small subset of patients, but this is a good starting point. Plus, it is not always ideal to do cross-trial comparisons. Still, the drop in stock price doesn't make sense from my perspective. The company reached the ≥30% uACR reduction threshold it needed for the whole patient population of AMKD patients and did better in FSGS patients [albeit a small sample size] than Vertex's inaxaplin study. Based on everything I laid out above, plus the fact that the data is good to move forward with for a pivotal study program for MZE829, it means that this program is still strong. I believe that that stock market reaction was overblown. Had this trial failed to reach the ≥30% uACR reduction threshold, then I would understand the reason for the drop, as it wouldn't have been a meaningful outcome in that case.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC Filing, Maze Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $360 million as of December 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is because the company was able to first obtain a total of $150 million before deducting expenses from an oversubscribed private placement agreement back in September of 2025. The truth is that this company has a lot of cash on hand. What makes me say that? Well, the truth is that there are several reasons why.

The first and most notable reason is the cash runway guidance it has given. It believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into 2028. Secondly, it actually has a 2026 ATM Open Market Sales Agreement with Jefferies LLC that it can use [established in February 2026], whereby it could from time to time offer and sell up to an aggregate of $200 million. To date, thus far, no shares have been sold under this agreement. The last reason is because it entered into a senior secured term loan facility with Hercules Capital Incorporated for an aggregate principal amount of $200 million.

As part of developing this term loan facility, Maze Therapeutics received an initial term loan amount of $40 million. There is potential to earn up to an additional six term loan tranches for up to an aggregate of $160 million through February 2031. However, in order to receive these other term loan tranches, there are certain conditions that have to be met. The company burns about $38.1 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down to $27.6 million in R&D expenses and then $10.5 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Maze Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive data that the company just obtained from its phase 2 HORIZON study using MZE829 for the treatment of patients with AMKD. The reason why is because the company intends to take the data it obtained thus far and initiate a pivotal program. The risk here is that even though the company established POC data showing a mean uACR reduction of 35.6% from the trial, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow the company to initiate a phase 3 clinical study using this drug to treat these AMKD patients.

The second risk lies with the fact that the company is not the only one developing an APOL1 inhibitor for the treatment of patients with AMKD. As I have shown above, Vertex Pharmaceuticals is in the process of evaluating the use of the small molecule inhibitor of APOL1, inaxaplin, in the ongoing phase 2/3 AMPLITUDE study to treat patients with AMKD. Vertex believes it is able to release 48-week interim results from this study either by the end of 2026 or early 2027. The risk here is that its drug could achieve a significantly better mean proteinuria reduction compared to what has been observed so far with MZE829. Thus, not only does Maze have to do well on its own in a potential phase 3 study, but its data is likely to be compared to that of the AMPLITUDE study from Vertex.

The third and final risk would be in terms of the additional shots on goal that this company has as part of its pipeline. Its other candidate, SLC6A19 inhibitor MZE782, is to be explored in two separate POC studies treating patients with PKU and CKD. Both of these mid-stage POC trials are expected to be initiated in 2026. The risk here is that even though MZE782 was able to achieve an initial eGFR dip of -11.5 mL/min/1.73 m^2 over seven days versus -2.5 for placebo for patients that took 240 mg of this drug twice daily, there is no guarantee that this will be carried over for an extended period of time in these other mid-stage trials.

Conclusion

I believe it's important to initiate Maze Therapeutics with a "Buy" rating for now. The reason why is because one can't say that the data was very bad. The company met the necessary threshold from its phase 2 HORIZON trial using MZE829 for the treatment of patients with AMKD. Again, the threshold was just to observe a mean uACR reduction of ≥30%. The company exceeded this expectation easily, and this is the first reason. Secondly, it's notable that despite only 4 FSGS patients being treated with MZE829, the drug did perform better than that of Vertex's study.

However, it's not prudent to do cross-trial comparisons anyways, but this gives an idea of where this drug is at in terms of targeting other subpopulations of AMKD patients. I still believe that the selloff on the stock was overblown. Maze not only did what it needed to, but it could also be in line to be able to initiate a pivotal late-stage study for this program of MZE829 treating these patients with AMKD. With everything I mentioned above, I'm initiating this stock with a "Buy" rating. Should Vertex produce significantly superior data, or should something change with the progress of MZE829, then I believe that it would be important to change the rating at such time.

data.title: EPS: A Quality ETF Based On An Earnings-Weighted Approach, But A Hold For Now
data.description:
With an investment structure that favors companies with large and high-margin businesses, the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Fund ETF (EPS) is arguably a fund with a quality tilt. This is a core characteristic of the fund that is reflected in its increased exposure to technology and communication services, especially big tech, if we take as a reference my previous article covering this fund from late 2024. Although EPS’s current portfolio may hold up well in the short term, supported by the financial strength and business resilience of its core holdings, ongoing uncertainties do not help, warranting a cautious stance at this moment. With that in mind, the coming sections will break down the fund’s portfolio and compare sector allocation, total returns, and volatility with the broader market and a peer group of ETFs.

ETF Description And Highlights

EPS is designed to provide exposure to profitable companies in the U.S. equity market. Using a rules-based methodology, the fund first filters out companies that have not generated positive earnings in each of the past four quarters. From this pool, the largest 500 companies by market cap are then weighted proportionally according to their earnings, subject to certain liquidity thresholds and a minimum price-to-earnings ratio of at least 2x, and with annual rebalances.

As of March 11, 2026, EPS had $1.3 billion in assets under management across a portfolio of 502 companies, with an average market capitalization of $410.1 billion. As much as a market-cap index like the Russell 1000, the fund shows a clear tilt toward the upper end of the market cap spectrum, with mega caps representing 46.1% of total assets and large caps 35.2%, which together account for more than 80% of the fund. This has changed little compared to the picture in October 2024. Still, there has been a small increase in the allocation to mega caps from 43.4% to 46.1%, while exposure to small caps dropped from 21.7% to 17.9%. This slight change makes sense, given the strong earnings growth delivered by mega caps over the last two years, especially big tech, which has led earnings growth in the equity market for much of the past few years. Thus, an increased allocation to mega caps is somewhat expected for an earnings-weighted fund like EPS.

EPS’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) (7.3%), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (6.0%), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) (5.6%), Apple Inc. (AAPL) (5.1%), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) (4.1%), Meta Platforms (3.7%), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) (2.0%), Berkshire Hathaway, Inc. (BRK.B) (1.9%), Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO) (1.6%), and Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) (1.5%), resembling to a large extent the holdings of broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000, where there is a large presence of giants from technology and communication services, given their strong earnings profile.

The following table includes a list of six ETFs blending value-oriented approaches and investment strategies driven by fundamentals, which will serve as the peer group for EPS in the analysis. The first is the Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV), by far the largest in the group, which tracks the CRSP US Large Cap Value Index with a traditional market-cap-weighted approach. A similar philosophy can be seen in the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), both relying on market-cap weighting while screening for value characteristics, with IWD leaning more into mid caps (30%). Employing a different angle, the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Large Company ETF (FNDX) and the Invesco RAFI US 1000 ETF (PRF) rely on fundamental metrics such as sales, cash flow, and dividends, and the VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat ETF (MOAT) tracks an equal-weighted index constituted of companies with sustainable competitive advantages and attractive valuations.

EPS’s largest sector is technology, which accounts for 31.3% of the fund. A second tier includes financial services (15.5%) and communication services (13.3%), healthcare (10.6%), and consumer discretionary (10.3%), while smaller allocations are spread across industrials (5.5%), consumer staples (4.6%), energy (4.4%), utilities (2.2%), basic materials (1.4%), and real estate (0.9%).

The fund has increased its allocation to technology by 4.8% since late 2024, in a sign of the stronger earnings growth from companies in the sector. At the same time, sectors like energy, industrials, consumer staples, and financial services had their weights reduced by the fund, as the fund needs to rebalance holdings to reflect its earnings-weighted structure. That said, EPS’s current sector mix has much in common with broader equity indexes like the Russell 1000, with only small allocation gaps in sectors like financial services (+3.2%), communication services (+2.8%), and industrials (-3.8%).

If we break down EPS’s holdings across its core sectors, we see that in technology, although the fund has nearly 75 positions in the sector, the bulk of the exposure is concentrated in a few names. Nvidia is by far the largest semiconductor player, followed by Broadcom, Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT), and Qualcomm. Apple and Microsoft are other large holdings with more than 9% of the fund, while the exposure to software is complemented by smaller positions in Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), Intuit Inc. (INTU), and Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), along with cybersecurity vendor Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW).

The exposure to financial services also includes more than 70 companies but is anchored by a few sub-industries, such as global banks through JPMorgan, Bank of America Corporation (BAC), Wells Fargo & Company (WFC), and Citigroup Inc. (C). The fund also owns the conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway and global payments players Visa, Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA), while holdings in insurance, such as The Allstate Corporation (ALL), MetLife, Inc. (MET), and Prudential Financial, Inc. (PRU), round out the sector.

Another large sector is communication services, but with only 16 holdings and heavy positions in Alphabet and Meta Platforms, two companies with sizable earnings.
